{{Event information
| Event name = Seihakusai
| image = File:Dekayama.JPG
| image description = One of the largest ''dekayama'' (float) from the Seihakusai Festival
| Type = {{ill|Festival|ja|祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭|1=Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132241}}
| Date = May
| Venue = {{ill|Ōjinushi Shrine|ja|大地主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjinushi Shrine|1=Otokone Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433686}}, {{ill|Nanao, Ishikawa|en|Nanao, Ishikawa|WD=Q695892|lt=Nanao|1=Nanao, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|WD=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}
}}
[[File:Nanao May 5 2006 crowds.jpg|thumb|Crowds at Seihakusai on May 5, 2006]]
'''Seihakusai''' (青柏祭) is the annual {{ill|Reisai|lt=Reisai|lt=Reitaisai|ja|例大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reitaisai|ja_comment=fixed the redirect|1=Reisai|2=ja|3=例祭|4=fr|5=Reisai|lt=Reitaisai}} of {{ill|Ōjinushi Shrine|ja|大地主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōjinushi Shrine|1=Otokone Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433686}}, held every year in {{ill|Nanao, Ishikawa|en|Nanao, Ishikawa|WD=Q695892|lt=Nanao|1=Nanao, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|WD=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}, from {{ill|May 1|en|May 1|WD=Q2544|lt=May 1|1=May 1}} to {{ill|May 5|en|May 5|WD=Q2550|lt=May 5|1=May 5}}. It is one of the {{ill|Four Major Festivals of Nanao|ja|七尾四大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Major Festivals of Nanao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} along with the {{ill|Okuma Kabuto Festival|ja|お熊甲祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okuma Kabuto Festival|qq=}}, {{ill|Mukōda Fire Festival|ja|向田の火祭り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukōda Fire Festival|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ishizaki Hōtō Festival|ja|石崎奉燈祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishizaki Hōtō Festival|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.nanao.lg.jp/kikaku-s/shise/sesaku/1sogokekaku/honpen/documents/kihonkeikaku_1_4_5.pdf|title=Basic Plan: Chapter 1, Arts and Culture|access-date=2024-08-04|publisher=Nanao City|page=88}}</ref>{{Refnest|group="Note"|The Ishizaki Hōtō Festival, {{ill|Nanao Gion Festival|ja|七尾祇園祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Gion Festival|1=Nanao Gion Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353501}}, and {{ill|Nanao Port Festival|ja|七尾港まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanao Port Festival|1=Nanao Port Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11353495}} are sometimes also included in the four major festivals.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://yamaguchi-ev.com/ishikawa-event/1692/|title=Events in Ishikawa Prefecture in May|access-date=2024-08-04|publisher=Yamaguchi Corporation}}</ref>}}

The festival is designated as a [[Nationally Designated Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]], and its ''dekayama'' floats are also registered as part of the {{ill|Intangible cultural heritage|en|Intangible cultural heritage|WD=Q84036549|lt=UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage|1=Intangible cultural heritage}}.

== Overview ==

The name "Seihakusai" comes from the tradition of offering {{ill|Shinsen|en|Shinsen|WD=Q11591045|lt=Shinsen|1=Shinsen}} on fresh oak leaves (''seihaku''). Each of the three mountain towns (''yamachō'')—{{ill|Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|en|Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)|WD=Q1204737|lt=Fuchū-chō|1=Fuchū, Hiroshima (town)}}, {{ill|Kaji-chō|ja|鍛冶町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaji-chō|1=Q11650753|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11650753}}, and {{ill|Uomachi|ja|魚町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uomachi|1=Q11674056|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674056}}—contributes one enormous float known as a ''dekayama.'' The name "dekayama" originates from the local word ''dekai'' (big) to describe the enormous size of the floats.<ref>[http://www.nanao-cci.or.jp/dekayama/event.html History of Seihakusai Dekayama Preservation Society]</ref>

During the festival, these colossal floats are pulled through the narrow streets of Nanao, barely clearing eaves and utility poles, with spectacular corner-turning performances. Visitors are welcome to help pull the floats during straight-line segments or turns. 

== History ==

The festival is said to have originated in {{ill|981 AD|ja|天元4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/981 AD|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, when {{ill|Minamoto no Shitagō|en|Minamoto no Shitagō|WD=Q1559382|lt=Minamoto no Shitagō|1=Minamoto no Shitagō}}, the governor of {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}}, designated it as the provincial festival. The current floats are believed to date back to the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, under the rule of {{ill|Hatakeyama Yoshitō|ja|畠山義統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatakeyama Yoshitō|1=Q11578686|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578686}}, though the exact origins remain unclear.<ref>[http://www7.ocn.ne.jp/~kita3079/seihakusai-dekayama-hozonkai-21.htm Nanao City Seihakusai Float Procession]</ref>

== Dekayama Floats ==

Each ''dekayama'' measures approximately 12 meters in height, 13 meters in length, and weighs around 20 tons, making them among the largest in Japan. Built without nails, the floats rely on traditional binding techniques using藤づる (wisteria vines). Each float is elaborately decorated with kabuki-themed dioramas, featuring hand-crafted figurines dressed in detailed costumes.

== Facilities Showcasing Seihakusai ==

* {{ill|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall|lt=Wakura Onsen Festival Hall|lt=Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall|ja|和倉温泉お祭り会館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall|qq=}} – A cultural center in {{ill|Wakura|ja|和倉町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakura|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 田鶴浜町|1=Tatsuruhama, Ishikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7688334}}, Nanao, dedicated to Nanao’s four major festivals, including Seihakusai, showcasing a full-scale replica of a ''dekayama.''
* {{ill|Noto Food Festival Market|ja|能登食祭市場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noto Food Festival Market|1=Noto Shokusai Ichiba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11612050}} – Features a display of a historic ''dekayama'' wheel at its entrance.
